hook: Whitewashed tomb of the city's blind hermit-saint, raised in 1881
facts: Current koubba built 1881 over the marabout's tomb at the north edge of the old medina
Sidi Belyout is the patron saint of Casablanca; the Sidi Belyout arrondissement carries his name
Called Abou Liyout, 'father of the lions'
Near Casa-Port station and the port gate on Boulevard Houphouet-Boigny
legend: Legend says he blinded himself to escape human corruption and went to live among wild beasts in the forest of Ain Sebaa
They say lions and gazelles obeyed him and he could speak with them
w: Legend says this hermit blinded himself rather than watch men corrupt themselves, then walked into the forest to live among lions and gazelles who supposedly obeyed his voice. They called him father of the lions. The whitewashed dome over his grave went up in 1881; the whole port district still bears his name. The city's quiet patron.

hook: A German merchant's neo-Moorish villa, now the museum of Casablanca's memory
facts: Built 1911-1913 for German merchant Carl Ficke by self-taught architect Ulysse Tonci
Among the first buildings on the heights of Mers Sultan, before Prost's plan
Served as a WWI detention centre, then an orphanage, then a school annex
Reopened February 2025 as Musee de la Memoire de Casablanca after restoration by Salima Naji
w: A German trader raised this neo-Moorish villa on the bare heights of Mers Sultan before the French town planners arrived. It then did time as a wartime detention centre, an orphanage, a school annex. Restored and reopened in 2025, it now tells the city's own story room by room, from prehistory to the School of Casa. Marble laid in Amazigh carpet patterns.

hook: Faux-minaret clock tower, demolished by the French and rebuilt from memory
facts: Original raised 1908-1910 by commander Dessigny, copying a tower he had built in Ain Sefra
One of the first French structures in Casablanca, on the old medina wall
Demolished in May 1948 for traffic and supposed fragility
Current near-identical copy rebuilt in 1993, rotated and shifted from the original spot
w: A French officer planted this square clock tower on the medina wall around 1909, copying one he'd built out in Ain Sefra; shaped like a minaret, it told colonial time over the old town. Knocked down in 1948 to free up traffic, then resurrected in 1993 a few metres off and twisted forty-five degrees. A monument to itself, rebuilt from memory.

hook: Stained glass and a crystal chandelier in the heart of Jewish Casablanca
facts: Built in 1949 by Algerian Jewish immigrants; refurbished 1997
Stained-glass windows depict the twelve tribes of Israel, in a Chagall-inspired idiom
Seats around 500; long the centrepiece of the city's synagogues
Casablanca once held more than thirty synagogues
w: Algerian Jewish refugees built this synagogue in 1949, and its glory shows overhead: stained glass for the twelve tribes throwing tinted light onto an enormous crystal chandelier until the whole hall swims in colour. Casablanca once counted thirty-odd synagogues; this one became the heart of them. A community's last grand room.

hook: Zellij, wrought iron and bilingual postboxes in a neo-Moorish palace of post
facts: Built 1918-1920 by architect Adrien Laforgue in a neo-Moorish style
First building completed on the new Place Administrative, now Place Mohammed V
Facade of stone columns, arches and bold mosaic; interior of zellij and wrought iron
Postboxes labelled in Arabic and French
w: First stone laid on the great colonial square, this post office set the tone for everything around it: arches on stone columns, a mosaic skin, zellij and ironwork indoors, postboxes lettered in Arabic and French. One contemporary swore it would make any Frenchman turn green with envy. Bureaucracy dressed as a palace.

hook: Labyrinth flea market where the black market meets the soldering iron
facts: Land named for leather merchant El Hadj Bouazza Ghallef, who died in 1905
Traders relocated here in 1986 after a fire destroyed their old market
Shifted from leather and junk to electronics, decoders and pirated goods
Long a hub for repairs, salvage and grey-market gear in the Maarif district
w: Named for a leather dealer dead since 1905, this warren took its present form when fire-burned traders were dumped here in 1986. The leather gave way to satellite decoders, then phones, then anything with a circuit board, half of it pirated. Men solder, salvage and haggle down alleys you can lose yourself in. The city's grey-market brain.

hook: 1922 slaughterhouses turned into a sprawling cultural factory
facts: Built 1922 by Ernest Desmarest and Albert Greslin in working-class Hay Mohammadi
Worked as municipal slaughterhouses until 2000; listed on the national monuments register
Reconverted from 2009 into the Fabrique Culturelle des Anciens Abattoirs
Site of some 53,700 square metres, modelled on Amsterdam's reclaimed industrial venues
w: Casablanca killed its livestock here from 1922 until the millennium, in a brick complex the size of a small district out in working-class Hay Mohammadi. Since 2009 the killing floors host street art, theatre and music instead, an Amsterdam-style cultural factory. The smell of blood gone, the youth moved in. Heritage with a hose-down.

hook: 1922 Art Deco hotel with bronze lions and Edith Piaf's handbags
facts: Built in 1922 under the Protectorate, blending Art Deco with Moroccan tilework and stucco
Lobby guarded by a pair of bronze lions and lamp-bearing statues
Original 1922 Otis lift still in place, plus period telephones and cash registers
Edith Piaf stayed here; some of her belongings remain on display
w: Bronze lions still flank the lobby, the 1922 Otis lift still grinds between floors, and the old telephones and cash registers sit where the Protectorate left them. Edith Piaf slept here; a few of her handbags linger in a case. Art Deco bones wrapped in Moroccan tile and stucco. A time capsule that never quite got around to closing.

hook: Restored Art Deco cine-theatre of red velvet and puckered leather doors
facts: Opened in 1953 as a combined cinema and theatre on the ex-rue Poincare
Hosted premieres and visiting film stars before falling into disrepair
Granted restoration funding in 2019; reopened with original Art Deco detail intact
Now run as a multipurpose cultural centre with screenings, talks and music
w: Opened in 1953 for both films and live shows, this hall pulled premieres and visiting stars, then sagged into ruin like most of Morocco's grand cinemas. Money in 2019 brought it back: red seats, striped drapes, puckered leather doors, bold deco lettering everywhere. Now it screens and stages again. One of the rare ones saved, not razed.
